summary: [deterministic] all deterministic checks passed for engineering-vllm-inference-blueprint | [judge] [fallback from codex] All mechanical success criteria pass (zero warnings, clippy clean, fmt clean, 31 tests pass, CI workflow present, config-driven vLLM endpoint, stderr draining, semaphore concurrency limiting). However, the code has a critical security gap in billing signature verification and a high-severity billing overcharge bug that must be fixed before production deployment.

findings.title: verify_spend_auth_signature recovers signer key but never checks identity
findings.body: The function recovers the ECDSA public key from the signature but assigns it to `_recovered` (underscore prefix = intentionally unused) and never compares it against the authorized spending key for the commitment. Any valid ECDSA signature from ANY private key will pass verification as long as it's not expired and structurally valid. This completely defeats the purpose of SpendAuth — an attacker can forge authorizations for any commitment by signing with their own key.
findings.evidence: billing.rs:225 — `let Ok(_recovered) = VerifyingKey::recover_from_prehash(digest.as_slice(), &signature, rid)` — recovered key is discarded. Must query getAccount(commitment) to obtain the authorized spendingKey and compare.

findings.title: authorize_and_claim ignores actual_amount, always claims full authorized amount
findings.body: server.rs:211-215 carefully computes `charge_amount = actual_cost.min(spend_auth.amount)` and passes it to `authorize_and_claim`. But billing.rs:76 declares the parameter as `_actual_amount: u64` (unused). The on-chain authorizeSpend call uses the SpendAuth struct which contains the original pre-authorized amount, not the actual cost. Operators always claim the maximum authorized amount regardless of actual token usage.
findings.evidence: billing.rs:76 — `_actual_amount: u64` parameter is ignored. server.rs:215 — `let charge_amount = actual_cost.min(...)` is computed but has no effect.

findings.title: run_inference job handler uses expect() instead of error propagation
findings.body: The on-chain job handler in lib.rs uses .expect() for vLLM HTTP failures and response parsing. If vLLM is temporarily unavailable or returns malformed JSON, the async task panics instead of returning an error through the Tangle job result. This could leave on-chain jobs in a stuck/failed state without a proper error result.
findings.evidence: lib.rs:89 — `.expect("vLLM request failed")`, lib.rs:91 — `.expect("vLLM response parse failed")`. Should use `?` or map to a TangleResult error.

findings.title: Streaming requests silently downgraded to non-streaming
findings.body: ChatCompletionRequest deserializes a `stream` field (with #[allow(dead_code)]) but vllm.rs:155 hardcodes `"stream": false` in the proxied request. Clients requesting streaming get a non-streaming response with no error or indication. Should either support streaming or reject stream:true with a clear error.
findings.evidence: server.rs:64 — `#[allow(dead_code)] pub stream: bool`, vllm.rs:155 — `"stream": false`

findings.title: Billing cost tests don't exercise BillingClient.calculate_cost directly
findings.body: Tests test_calculate_cost_basic/zero/large manually compute `field * price` instead of calling `BillingClient::calculate_cost()`. Only test_billing_client_new actually calls the method. If calculate_cost logic changed (e.g., added rounding), the manual tests would still pass while the method broke. These tests verify arithmetic, not the actual method.
findings.evidence: billing.rs:293-296 — `let input_cost = 100u64 * billing.price_per_input_token` vs calling calculate_cost(100, 50)

findings.title: No test for verify_spend_auth_signature with tampered data
findings.body: The real-signature test (test_verify_with_real_signature) proves a valid sig verifies, but there's no test that modifies one field (e.g., changes the amount or operator after signing) and asserts rejection. This would catch the critical finding above — the current test passes even with the broken verification because the function only checks structural validity.
findings.evidence: billing.rs:434-519 — only positive path tested. Need a test that signs with key A then changes operator/amount and asserts false.
